story:
Everyone around here has a different theory about how I landed my hooman arrangement, and they’re all wrong. Daddy swears I escaped a bloodhound farm. My cub-brother Randy insists he found me. Mama likes to say Hershey led me here because he knew they needed someone like me. Mama’s version feels closest.

See, Hershey ran a night-time detective agency. He brought me on a couple years back. When he knew he was getting ready to move on, he asked me to take over—not just the stakeouts and sneaking, but the real mission: protecting his hoomans. “I’m telling you, brother—they’ve got a squirrel problem. Danger every day, and they don’t even see it. They’re good people, though. They’ll feed you, let you sleep inside, rub your belly, and give you all the butt scratches you can handle.”

“Oh, I can handle a lot,” I told him. “Table food, or just kibble?”

“Both. And if you don’t mind me saying, you look like you haven’t been eating much anyway—are you picky?”

“Nope. Just wanted to know what to expect.”

“Trust me—it’s a good arrangement. Will you take it?”

“As sure as I’ve ever been about anything,” I said, and then he cried. I’d never seen Hershey cry. That’s when I knew this was serious. He wanted his people safe, and he didn’t want the business to die with him. So we hatched a plan.

“Watch for the middle cub, Randy,” Hershey said. “He skateboards at night with another juvenile hooman.” That night was the last time I ever saw my friend. Whether there’s a rainbow bridge or not, I hope he crossed it to a place he deserves. After a few days with no sign of him, I knew it was time. I went out.

I waited in the dark, sniffing and listening. I smelled cologne before I heard those skateboards. Holy bork, they’re loud. The cubs laughed, zoomed past, and I slipped out of the shadows to follow—close enough to be seen, far enough not to spook them. They stopped under a street light. I stopped in the glow, and remembered Hershey’s tip: “Open your mouth and let your tongue hang out—the humans think you’re smiling.” Every dog knows panting’s for cooling down, but fine. I put on the big goofy grin. Tongue lolling (mine is impressively long, by the way). Worked like a charm.

“Awwww, look at that big dog! He’s following us! C’mere, boy!”

I obliged and gave them a thorough sniff. Not just affection—a full intel sweep. One had spaghetti for dinner, the other chicken. Both had been around female dogs: a poodle and a Wheaten. Ah-ha. Hershey’s pack sister was a Wheaten. So this cub was his. The one I needed to impress. I cranked the sniffer to theatrical levels: sniiiiffff, sniiiifffff, snifffffff. Cub magic.

“Hey, where’s your collar?” they asked. I gave them my best doggy smirk. I thought you’d never ask.

They worried I’d get hit by a car and talked about a spare collar and leash to find my owner. Sweet, if not exactly a brilliant nighttime plan. I already knew nobody would claim me. I didn’t live in a hooman house—yet.

Winning over the adults was easy, just like Hershey predicted. A few strategic sniffs, a couple of star-quality “smiles,” and they were mine. The Wheaten, Mandy, was another story—again, dead-on Hershey: “She’s going to hate you at first.” She met me with fire: “Who are you, big idiot? This is MY house and MY hoomans! I will literally bite your throat.” I just laughed inside. She had backbone, sharp eyes—perfect secretary material.

So that’s how I took the case, took the house, and took the job Hershey trusted me with: guarding my new pack from squirrels, danger, and everything they can’t smell.
